When we look at the history of legislation in Islam, Mecca and Medina, those are two very different eras. This is not a reference to the place where these chapters were revealed. When the scholars say this chapter is mechi or this chapter is Medani, it's not a reference to the place. It's very important because a lot of things changed between Mecca and Medina because of those who the verses and chapters were speaking to predominantly in Mecca as opposed to Medina. That's right after the ninth year. What was the ninth year? And so Allah never takes something without replacing it with something equal or better if it is not being taken as a punishment. But overall, those were the only two pillars of Islam in Mecca. In Medina, the rest of the pillars of Islam were commanded. And also the rules and laws and regulations of marriage, the rules and laws and regulations of transactions, of sales, of ownership, of hud of punishment. All of these things were detailed and began in Medina. And so it's important for us to know what was the first thing of that era that was revealed. They are predominantly Muslim or they are on the verge of becoming Muslim. Allah tells us those who when they seek their rights from others, they want them completely. Not one little right missing, not one grain of rice, not one morsel of salt, not one thing missing. I want my money's worth. But when it's time for them to give the rights of others, they only give the bare minimum and they take whatever extra they can get. The scholars tell us that the Arab linguists tell us that al- is taken from the root word And or is the extra little amount that is found on top of a heap when its volume is measured. So let me give you an example of that. Let's say we take a a a cup of rice, right? Some of the middle of that cup, if it's completely full, will go past the top a little bit, right? That heap on top. And if you wipe your hand over the top of it and level it with the rim of the cup, that extra that comes off the top, that is referred to as a. And so a is someone who eagerly and unremittently seeks that extra. I need to have that and I will sneak it in wherever I can and I will also sneak it out of whoever I have to give it to. This is where the essence of this word comes from. So a maf is a person who seeks his full rights from others, but he as long as he can and get away with it, he or she will take away or deplete the rights of others for their own gain. summary: This lecture discusses Surah Al-Mutaffifin, the first chapter revealed to Prophet Muhammad in Medina, emphasizing the critical transition between the Meccan and Medinan periods of Islam. The speaker explains the linguistic and theological meaning of "mutaffifin" (those who deal in fraud), describing individuals who demand their full rights from others while refusing to give others their due rights. The sermon connects this Quranic concept to contemporary Muslim attitudes, particularly the tendency to focus on personal entitlements rather than personal responsibilities. The speaker critiques how Muslims obsess over what they are owed rather than what they owe, drawing parallels to hypocritical behavior and emphasizing that true faith requires fulfilling others' rights even when our own rights are violated.
suggested_title: Surah Al-Mutaffifin: Fulfilling the Rights of Others Over Our Own
catchy_title: Why We Demand Rights But Dodge Responsibilities
